[Papuan](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinean_art "Papua New Guinean art") |

refers to art produced in [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") between the 6th millennium BC and the 4th century AD, spanning from [Prehistoric Egypt](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt") until [the Christianization](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great_and_Christianity "Constantine the Great and Christianity") of [Roman Egypt](/wiki/Roman_Egypt "Roman Egypt"). It includes paintings, sculptures, drawings on papyrus, [faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience"), jewelry, ivories, architecture, and other art media. It was a conservative tradition whose style changed very little over time. Much of the surviving examples comes from tombs and monuments, giving insight into the [ancient Egyptian afterlife beliefs](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_afterlife_beliefs "Ancient Egyptian afterlife beliefs").

**Ancient Egyptian art** refers to art produced in [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") between the 6th millennium BC and the 4th century AD, spanning from [Prehistoric Egypt](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt") until [the Christianization](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great_and_Christianity "Constantine the Great and Christianity") of [Roman Egypt](/wiki/Roman_Egypt "Roman Egypt"). It includes paintings, sculptures, drawings on papyrus, [faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience"), jewelry, ivories, architecture, and other art media. It was a conservative tradition whose style changed very little over time. Much of the surviving examples comes from tombs and monuments, giving insight into the [ancient Egyptian afterlife beliefs](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_afterlife_beliefs "Ancient Egyptian afterlife beliefs").

The [ancient Egyptian language](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_language "Ancient Egyptian language") had no word for "art". Artworks served an essentially functional purpose that was bound with [religion](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_religion "Ancient Egyptian religion") and [ideology](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology").[[1]](#cite_note-1) To render a subject in art was to grant it permanence; thus, ancient Egyptian art portrayed an idealized and unrealistic version of the world. There was no significant tradition of individual artistic expression since art served a wider and cosmic purpose of maintaining order ([Ma'at](/wiki/Ma%27at "Ma'at")).

## Art of Pre-Dynastic Egypt (6000–3000 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Art of Pre-Dynastic Egypt (6000–3000 BC)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Predynastic_collage_%28new_version%29.jpg/330px-Predynastic_collage_%28new_version%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Predynastic_collage_(new_version).jpg)

Artifacts of Egypt from the Prehistoric period, 4400–3100 BC: clockwise from top left: a Badarian ivory figurine, a Naqada jar, a [Bat](/wiki/Bat_(goddess) "Bat (goddess)") figurine, a [cosmetic palette](/wiki/Cosmetic_palette "Cosmetic palette"), a [flint knife](/wiki/Gebel_el-Arak_Knife "Gebel el-Arak Knife"), and a [diorite](/wiki/Diorite "Diorite") vase.

Pre-Dynastic Egypt, corresponding to the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") period of the [prehistory of Egypt](/wiki/Prehistory_of_Egypt "Prehistory of Egypt"), spanned from c. 6000 BC to the beginning of the [Early Dynastic Period](/wiki/Early_Dynastic_Period_(Egypt) "Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)"), around 3100 BC.

Continued expansion of the desert forced the early ancestors of the [Egyptians](/wiki/Egyptians "Egyptians") to settle around the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile") and adopt a more sedentary lifestyle during the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic"). The period from 9000 to 6000 BC has left very little archaeological evidence, but around 6000 BC, Neolithic settlements began to appear all over Egypt.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford1992[httpsarchiveorgdetailsegyptcanaanisrae00redfpage6_6]-2) Studies based on [morphological](/wiki/Morphology_(biology) "Morphology (biology)"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) [genetic](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) and [archaeological](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") data[[5]](#cite_note-5) have attributed these settlements to migrants from the [Fertile Crescent](/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent") returning during the [Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution"), bringing [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") to the region.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Merimde culture (5000–4200 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Merimde culture (5000–4200 BC)")]

From about 5000 to 4200 BC, the [Merimde culture](/wiki/Merimde_culture "Merimde culture"), known only from a large settlement site at the edge of the Western Nile Delta, flourished in Lower Egypt.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The culture has strong connections to the [Faiyum A culture](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt#Faiyum_A_culture "Prehistoric Egypt") as well as the Levant. People lived in small huts, produced simple undecorated pottery, and had stone tools. Cattle, sheep, goats, and pigs were raised, and wheat, sorghum and barley were planted. The Merimde people buried their dead within the settlement and produced clay figurines.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The first Egyptian life-size head made of clay comes from Merimde.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Badarian culture (4400–4000 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Badarian culture (4400–4000 BC)")]

The [Badarian culture](/wiki/Badarian_culture "Badarian culture"), from about 4400 to 4000 BC,[[9]](#cite_note-Shaw_479-9) is named for the [Badari](/wiki/Badari "Badari") site near Der Tasa. It followed the [Tasian culture](/wiki/Tasian_culture "Tasian culture") (c. 4500 BC) but was so similar that many consider them one continuous period. The Badarian culture continued to produce blacktop-ware pottery (albeit much improved in quality) and was assigned [sequence dating](/wiki/Sequence_dating "Sequence dating") (SD) numbers 21–29.[[10]](#cite_note-Gardiner_389-10) The primary difference that prevents scholars from merging the two periods is that Badarian sites use copper in addition to stone and are thus [chalcolithic](/wiki/Chalcolithic "Chalcolithic") settlements, while the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") Tasian sites are still considered [Stone Age](/wiki/Stone_Age "Stone Age").[[10]](#cite_note-Gardiner_389-10)

### Naqada culture (4000–3000 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Naqada culture (4000–3000 BC)")]

The [Naqada culture](/wiki/Naqada_culture "Naqada culture") is an archaeological culture of [Chalcolithic](/wiki/Chalcolithic "Chalcolithic") [Predynastic Egypt](/wiki/Predynastic_Egypt "Predynastic Egypt") (c. 4400–3000 BC), named for the town of [Naqada](/wiki/Naqada "Naqada"), [Qena Governorate](/wiki/Qena_Governorate "Qena Governorate"). It is divided into three sub-periods: Naqada I, II and III.

#### Naqada I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Naqada I")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Vase_with_human_figures_%28front%29._Naqada._Royal_Museums_of_Art_and_History%2C_Brussels.jpg/120px-Vase_with_human_figures_%28front%29._Naqada._Royal_Museums_of_Art_and_History%2C_Brussels.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vase_with_human_figures_(front)._Naqada._Royal_Museums_of_Art_and_History,_Brussels.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/E3002_vessel%2C_Naqada_period_%28compacted%29.jpg/250px-E3002_vessel%2C_Naqada_period_%28compacted%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:E3002_vessel,_Naqada_period_(compacted).jpg)

Vase with victorious human figure, and smaller individuals attached with a rope at the neck. Naqada IC-IIA (c. 3800-3600 BC). Royal Museums of Art and History, E3002.[[11]](#cite_note-Global-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The [Naqada I](/wiki/Naqada_I "Naqada I") (Amratian) culture lasted from about 4000 to 3500 BC.[[9]](#cite_note-Shaw_479-9) Black-topped ware continues to appear, but white cross-line ware – a type of pottery which has been decorated with crossing sets of close parallel white lines – is also found at this time. The Amratian period falls between 30 and 39 SD.[[13]](#cite_note-Gardiner_390-13)

#### Naqada II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Naqada II")]

The Gebel el-Arak Knife (3200 BC)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Gebel_el-Arak_Knife_ivory_handle_%28front%29.jpg/120px-Gebel_el-Arak_Knife_ivory_handle_%28front%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gebel_el-Arak_Knife_ivory_handle_(front).jpg)

Egyptian prehistoric [Gebel el-Arak Knife](/wiki/Gebel_el-Arak_Knife "Gebel el-Arak Knife") ivory handle (back), [Abydos](/wiki/Abydos,_Egypt "Abydos, Egypt"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"). [Louvre Museum](/wiki/Louvre_Museum "Louvre Museum").[[14]](#cite_note-louvre1-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Gebel_el-Arak_Knife_ivory_handle_%28front_top_part_detail%29.jpg/250px-Gebel_el-Arak_Knife_ivory_handle_%28front_top_part_detail%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gebel_el-Arak_Knife_ivory_handle_(front_top_part_detail).jpg)

[Mesopotamian](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") king as [Master of Animals](/wiki/Master_of_Animals "Master of Animals") on the [Gebel el-Arak Knife](/wiki/Gebel_el-Arak_Knife "Gebel el-Arak Knife"). This work of art both shows the influence of Mesopotamia on [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") at an early date, during a period of [Egypt–Mesopotamia relations](/wiki/Egypt-Mesopotamia_relations "Egypt-Mesopotamia relations"), and the state of Mesopotamian royal iconography during the Uruk period.[[14]](#cite_note-louvre1-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The [Naqada II](/wiki/Naqada_II "Naqada II") (Gerzean) culture from about 3500 to 3200 BC,[[9]](#cite_note-Shaw_479-9) is named after the site of [Gerzeh](/wiki/Gerzeh "Gerzeh"). It was the next stage in Egyptian cultural development, and it was during this time that the foundation of Dynastic Egypt was laid. Naqada II culture is largely an unbroken development of [Naqada I](/wiki/Naqada_I "Naqada I"), expanding from the Upper Nile region towards the Nile delta and towards Nubia.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford199216-16) Naqada II pottery has been assigned SD values of 40 through 62, and is distinctly different from Naqada I white cross-lined wares or black-topped ware.[[13]](#cite_note-Gardiner_390-13) It was painted mostly in dark red with pictures of animals, people, and ships, as well as geometric symbols that appear to have been derived from animals.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford199216-16) Wavy handles, which were rare before this period (though occasionally found as early as SD 35), became more common and more elaborate until they were almost completely ornamental.[[13]](#cite_note-Gardiner_390-13)

During this period, distinctly foreign objects and art forms entered Egypt, indicating contact with several parts of Asia, particularly with Mesopotamia. Objects such as the [Gebel el-Arak Knife](/wiki/Gebel_el-Arak_Knife "Gebel el-Arak Knife") handle, which has patently Mesopotamian relief carvings on it, have been found in Egypt,[[17]](#cite_note-17) and the silver which appears in this period can only have been obtained from [Asia Minor](/wiki/Asia_Minor "Asia Minor").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford199216-16) In addition, Egyptian objects were created which clearly mimic Mesopotamian forms.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford199218-18) Cylinder seals appeared in Egypt, as well as recessed paneling architecture. The Egyptian reliefs on [cosmetic palettes](/wiki/Cosmetic_palette "Cosmetic palette") were made in the same style as the contemporary Mesopotamian [Uruk culture](/wiki/Uruk_culture "Uruk culture"), and ceremonial mace heads from the late Gerzean and early Semainean were crafted in the Mesopotamian "pear-shaped" style, instead of the Egyptian native style.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford199217-19)

The route of this trade is difficult to determine, but contact with [Canaan](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan") does not predate the early dynastic, so it is usually assumed to have been by water.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford199222-20) During the time when the Dynastic Race Theory was popular, it was theorized that Uruk sailors circumnavigated [Arabia](/wiki/Arabia "Arabia"), but a [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") route, probably by middlemen through [Byblos](/wiki/Byblos "Byblos"), is more likely, as evidenced by the presence of [Byblian](/wiki/Byblos "Byblos") objects in Egypt.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford199222-20)

The fact that so many Naqada II sites are at the mouths of [wadis](/wiki/Wadi "Wadi") which lead to the Red Sea may indicate some amount of trade via the Red Sea (though Byblian trade potentially could have crossed the Sinai and then taken to the Red Sea).[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford199220-21) Also, it is considered unlikely that something as complicated as recessed panel architecture could have worked its way into Egypt by proxy, and at least a small contingent of migrants is often suspected.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford199222-20)

Despite this evidence of foreign influence, Egyptologists generally agree that the Naqada II Culture is predominantly indigenous to Egypt.

#### Protodynastic Period (Naqada III)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Protodynastic Period (Naqada III)")]

Main article: [Naqada III](/wiki/Naqada_III "Naqada III")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Tell_el-Farkha_golden_figurine_%2860cm_tall%29.jpg/120px-Tell_el-Farkha_golden_figurine_%2860cm_tall%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tell_el-Farkha_golden_figurine_(60cm_tall).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Male_figurine_from_Tell_el-Farkha._Late_Predynastic_period_%28Naqada_IIIB%2C_c._3200%E2%80%933000_BCE%29._Egyptian_Museum_%28Cairo%29._30cm_height_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Male_figurine_from_Tell_el-Farkha._Late_Predynastic_period_%28Naqada_IIIB%2C_c._3200%E2%80%933000_BCE%29._Egyptian_Museum_%28Cairo%29._30cm_height_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Male_figurine_from_Tell_el-Farkha._Late_Predynastic_period_(Naqada_IIIB,_c._3200%E2%80%933000_BCE)._Egyptian_Museum_(Cairo)._30cm_height_(cropped).jpg)

Oldest known representations of ancient Egyptian rulers, from [Tell el-Farkha](/wiki/Tell_el-Farkha "Tell el-Farkha"). Naqada IIIB, c. 3200–3000 BC.[[22]](#cite_note-MUS-22)[[23]](#cite_note-PER-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The Naqada III period, from about 3200 to 3000 BC,[[9]](#cite_note-Shaw_479-9) is generally taken to be identical with the [Protodynastic](/wiki/Protodynastic_Period_of_Egypt "Protodynastic Period of Egypt") period, during which Egypt was unified.

Naqada III is notable for being the first era with [hieroglyphs](/wiki/Hieroglyphs "Hieroglyphs") (though this is disputed), the first regular use of [serekhs](/wiki/Serekh "Serekh"), the first irrigation, and the first appearance of royal cemeteries.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The art of the Naqada III period was quite sophisticated, exemplified by [cosmetic palettes](/wiki/Cosmetic_palette "Cosmetic palette"). These were used in [predynastic Egypt](/wiki/Predynastic_Egypt "Predynastic Egypt") to grind and apply ingredients for facial or body cosmetics. By the Protodynastic period, the decorative palettes appear to have lost this function and were instead commemorative, ornamental, and possibly ceremonial. They were made almost exclusively from [siltstone](/wiki/Siltstone "Siltstone"), which originated from quarries in the [Wadi Hammamat](/wiki/Wadi_Hammamat "Wadi Hammamat"). Many of the palettes were found at [Hierakonpolis](/wiki/Hierakonpolis "Hierakonpolis"), a center of power in predynastic Upper Egypt. After the unification of the country, the palettes ceased to be included in tomb assemblages.

## Art of Dynastic Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Art of Dynastic Egypt")]

### Early Dynastic Period (3100–2685 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Early Dynastic Period (3100–2685 BC)")]

See also: [Early Dynastic Period of Egypt](/wiki/Early_Dynastic_Period_of_Egypt "Early Dynastic Period of Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Narmer_palette_%28obverse%29.jpg/250px-Narmer_palette_%28obverse%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Narmer_palette_(obverse).jpg)

[Narmer Palette](/wiki/Narmer_Palette "Narmer Palette")

The [Early Dynastic Period of Egypt](/wiki/Early_Dynastic_Period_of_Egypt "Early Dynastic Period of Egypt") immediately follows the unification of [Upper and Lower Egypt](/wiki/Upper_and_Lower_Egypt "Upper and Lower Egypt"), c. 3100 BC. It is generally taken to include the [First](/wiki/First_Dynasty_of_Egypt "First Dynasty of Egypt") and [Second Dynasties](/wiki/Second_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Second Dynasty of Egypt"), lasting from the end of the Naqada III archaeological period until about 2686 BC, or the beginning of the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Old Kingdom of Egypt").[[9]](#cite_note-Shaw_479-9)

[Cosmetic palettes](/wiki/Cosmetic_palette "Cosmetic palette") reached a new level of sophistication during this period, in which the Egyptian writing system also experienced further development. Initially, Egyptian writing was composed primarily of a few symbols denoting amounts of various substances. In the cosmetic palettes, symbols were used together with pictorial descriptions. By the end of the Third Dynasty, this had been expanded to include more than 200 symbols, both [phonograms](/wiki/Phonogram_(linguistics) "Phonogram (linguistics)") and [ideograms](/wiki/Ideogram "Ideogram").[[28]](#cite_note-Kinnaer-28)

### Old Kingdom (2686–2181 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Old Kingdom (2686–2181 BC)")]

Main article: [Old Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Old Kingdom of Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Princess_Nefertiabet_%28portrait_detail%29_in_her_Giza_tomb._Reign_of_Kheops_%282590-2565_BCE%29._Louvre_Museum_E_15591.jpg/250px-Princess_Nefertiabet_%28portrait_detail%29_in_her_Giza_tomb._Reign_of_Kheops_%282590-2565_BCE%29._Louvre_Museum_E_15591.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princess_Nefertiabet_(portrait_detail)_in_her_Giza_tomb._Reign_of_Kheops_(2590-2565_BCE)._Louvre_Museum_E_15591.jpg)

Princess [Nefertiabet](/wiki/Nefertiabet "Nefertiabet"), likely daughter of [Khufu](/wiki/Khufu "Khufu"), from her Giza tomb (2590-2565 BC). Louvre Museum E 15591.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The [Old Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Old Kingdom of Egypt") is the period spanning c. 2686–2181 BC. It is also known as the "Age of the Pyramids" or the "Age of the Pyramid Builders", as it encompasses the reigns of the great pyramid builders of the [Fourth Dynasty](/wiki/Fourth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Fourth Dynasty of Egypt"). King [Sneferu](/wiki/Sneferu "Sneferu") perfected the art of [pyramid](/wiki/Pyramid "Pyramid")-building and the [pyramids of Giza](/wiki/Pyramids_of_Giza "Pyramids of Giza") were constructed under the kings [Khufu](/wiki/Khufu "Khufu"), [Khafre](/wiki/Khafre "Khafre") and [Menkaure](/wiki/Menkaure "Menkaure").[[35]](#cite_note-35) [Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") attained its first sustained peak of civilization, the first of three so-called "Kingdom" [periods](/wiki/Egyptian_chronology "Egyptian chronology") (followed by the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt") and [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt")) which mark the high points of civilization in the lower [Nile Valley](/wiki/Nile_Valley "Nile Valley").

### Middle Kingdom (c. 2055–1650 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Middle Kingdom (c. 2055–1650 BC)")]

Main article: [Middle Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Aswan%2C_Egypt_WestBankTombs_2007jan15._14_byDanielCsorfoly.JPG/250px-Aswan%2C_Egypt_WestBankTombs_2007jan15._14_byDanielCsorfoly.JPG)](/wiki/File:Aswan,_Egypt_WestBankTombs_2007jan15._14_byDanielCsorfoly.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Nubian_Archers.jpg/250px-Nubian_Archers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nubian_Archers.jpg)

The [Middle Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt") (a.k.a. "the Period of Reunification") is marked by political division known as the [First Intermediate Period](/wiki/First_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "First Intermediate Period of Egypt"). The Middle Kingdom lasted from around 2050 BC to around 1710 BC, from the reunification of Egypt under the reign of [Mentuhotep II](/wiki/Mentuhotep_II "Mentuhotep II") of the [Eleventh Dynasty](/wiki/Eleventh_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Eleventh Dynasty of Egypt") to the end of the [Twelfth Dynasty](/wiki/Twelfth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt"). The Eleventh Dynasty ruled from [Thebes](/wiki/Thebes,_Egypt "Thebes, Egypt") and the Twelfth Dynasty ruled from [el-Lisht](/wiki/Lisht "Lisht"). During the Middle Kingdom period, [Osiris](/wiki/Osiris "Osiris") became the most important deity in [popular religion](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_religion#Popular_religion "Ancient Egyptian religion").[[37]](#cite_note-37) The Middle Kingdom was followed by the [Second Intermediate Period of Egypt](/wiki/Second_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "Second Intermediate Period of Egypt"), another period of division that involved foreign invasions of the country by the [Hyksos](/wiki/Hyksos "Hyksos") of West Asia.

After the reunification of Egypt in the Middle Kingdom, the kings of the Eleventh and Twelfth Dynasties were able to return their focus to art. In the Eleventh Dynasty, the king's monuments were made in a style influenced by the Memphite models of the Fifth and early Sixth Dynasties and the pre-unification Theban relief style all but disappeared. These changes had an ideological purpose, as the Eleventh Dynasty kings were establishing a centralized state, and returning to the political ideals of the Old Kingdom.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobins200890-38) In the early Twelfth Dynasty, the artwork had a uniformity of style due to the influence of the royal workshops. It was at this point that the quality of artistic production for the elite members of society reached a high point that was never surpassed, although it was equaled during other periods.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobins2008109-39) Egypt's prosperity in the late Twelfth Dynasty was reflected in the quality of the materials used for royal and private monuments.

### Second Intermediate Period (c. 1650–1550 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Second Intermediate Period (c. 1650–1550 BC)")]

Main articles: [Second Intermediate Period of Egypt](/wiki/Second_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "Second Intermediate Period of Egypt") and [Hyksos](/wiki/Hyksos "Hyksos")

The so-called "Hyksos sphinxes"

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Hyksos_Sphinxes.jpg/330px-Hyksos_Sphinxes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hyksos_Sphinxes.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Sphinx_Amenemhat3_Budge.jpg/250px-Sphinx_Amenemhat3_Budge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sphinx_Amenemhat3_Budge.jpg)

The so-called "[Hyksos sphinxes](/wiki/Tanite_sphinxes "Tanite sphinxes")" are peculiar sphinxes of [Amenemhat III](/wiki/Amenemhat_III "Amenemhat III") which were reinscribed by several Hyksos rulers, including [Apepi](/wiki/Apepi_(pharaoh) "Apepi (pharaoh)"). Earlier Egyptologists thought these were the faces of actual Hyksos rulers.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEel-Shahawy2005160-45)

The [Hyksos](/wiki/Hyksos "Hyksos"), a dynasty of rulers originating from the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"), do not appear to have produced any court art,[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBietak1999379-46) instead appropriating monuments from earlier dynasties by writing their names on them. Many of these are inscribed with the name of King [Khyan](/wiki/Khyan "Khyan").[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMüller2018212-47) A large palace at [Avaris](/wiki/Avaris "Avaris") has been uncovered, built in the Levantine rather than the Egyptian style, most likely by Khyan.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBard2015213-48) King [Apepi](/wiki/Apepi_(pharaoh) "Apepi (pharaoh)") is known to have patronized Egyptian scribal culture, commissioning the copying of the [Rhind Mathematical Papyrus](/wiki/Rhind_Mathematical_Papyrus "Rhind Mathematical Papyrus").[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_de_Mieroop2021151–153-49) The stories preserved in the [Westcar Papyrus](/wiki/Westcar_Papyrus "Westcar Papyrus") may also date from his reign.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERedford1992122-50)

The so-called "[Hyksos sphinxes](/wiki/Tanite_sphinxes "Tanite sphinxes")" or "Tanite sphinxes" are a group of royal sphinxes depicting the earlier Pharaoh [Amenemhat III](/wiki/Amenemhat_III "Amenemhat III") (Twelfth Dynasty) with some unusual traits compared to conventional statuary, for example prominent cheekbones and the thick mane of a lion, instead of the traditional [nemes](/wiki/Nemes "Nemes") headcloth. The name "Hyksos sphinxes" was given due to the fact that these were later reinscribed by several of the Hyksos kings, and were initially thought to represent the Hyksos kings themselves. Nineteenth-century scholars attempted to use the statues' features to assign a racial origin to the Hyksos.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECandelora201854-51) These sphinxes were seized by the Hyksos from cities of the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt") and then transported to their capital Avaris where they were reinscribed with the names of their new owners and adorned their palace.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEel-Shahawy2005160-45) Seven of those sphinxes are known, all from [Tanis](/wiki/Tanis "Tanis"), and now mostly located in the [Cairo Museum](/wiki/Cairo_Museum "Cairo Museum").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEel-Shahawy2005160-45)[[52]](#cite_note-52) [Other statues of Amenehat III](/wiki/File:Statues_of_Shepherd_Kings_by_Boston_Public_Library.jpg "File:Statues of Shepherd Kings by Boston Public Library.jpg") were found in Tanis and are associated with the Hyksos in the same manner.

### New Kingdom (c. 1550–1069 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: New Kingdom (c. 1550–1069 BC)")]

Main article: [New Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt")

The [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt"), also referred to as the "Egyptian Empire", is the period between the 16th and 11th centuries BC, covering the [18th](/wiki/Eighteenth_dynasty_of_Egypt "Eighteenth dynasty of Egypt"), [19th](/wiki/Nineteenth_dynasty_of_Egypt "Nineteenth dynasty of Egypt"), and [20th dynasties of Egypt](/wiki/Twentieth_dynasty_of_Egypt "Twentieth dynasty of Egypt"). The New Kingdom followed the [Second Intermediate Period](/wiki/Second_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "Second Intermediate Period of Egypt") and was succeeded by the [Third Intermediate Period](/wiki/Third_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "Third Intermediate Period of Egypt"). It was [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt")'s most prosperous time and marked the peak of its power.[[62]](#cite_note-:0-62) This tremendous wealth can be attributed to the centralization of bureaucratic power and many successful military campaigns which opened trade routes. With the expansion of the Egyptian Empire, Kings gained access to important commodities such as cedar from Lebanon and luxury materials such as [lapis lazuli](/wiki/Lapis_lazuli "Lapis lazuli") and [turquoise](/wiki/Turquoise "Turquoise").

The artwork produced during the New Kingdom falls into three broad periods: Pre-Amarna, Amarna, and [Ramesside](/wiki/Ramesside_Period "Ramesside Period"). Although stylistic changes as a result of shifts in power and variation of religious ideals occurred, the statuary and relief work throughout the New Kingdom continued to embody the main principles of Egyptian art: frontality and axiality, hierarchy of scale, and composite composition.

#### Pre-Amarna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Pre-Amarna")]

The Pre-Amarna period, the beginning of the eighteenth dynasty of the New Kingdom, was marked by the growing power of Egypt as an expansive empire. The artwork reflects a combination of Middle Kingdom techniques and subjects with the newly accessed materials and styles of foreign lands.[[63]](#cite_note-:5-63) A large portion of the art and architecture of the Pre-Amarna period was produced by the female king Hatshepshut, who led a widespread building campaign to all gods during her reign from 1473 to 1458 BC. She made significant additions to the temple at [Karnak](/wiki/Karnak "Karnak"), undertook the construction of an extensive mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahri, and produced a prolific amount of statuary and relief work in hard stone. The extent of these building projects was made possible by the centralization of power in Thebes and reopening of trade routes by previous New Kingdom ruler Ahmose I.[[64]](#cite_note-:4-64)

Hatshepsut's elaborate mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahri provides many well-preserved examples of the artwork produced during the Pre-Amarna period. The massive three-level, colonnaded temple was built into the cliffs of Thebes and adorned with extensive painted relief. Subjects of these reliefs ranged from traditional funerary images and legitimization of Hatshepsut as the divine ruler of Egypt to battle and expedition scenes in foreign lands. The temple also housed numerous statues of the female king and gods, particularly Amun-ra, some of which were colossal in scale. The artwork from Hatshepshut's reign is trademarked by the re-integration of Northern culture and style as a result of the reunification of Egypt. Thutmoses III, the predecessor to Hatshepsut, also commissioned vast amounts of large-scale artwork and by his death Egypt was the most powerful empire in the world.[[64]](#cite_note-:4-64)

##### State-Sponsored Temples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: State-Sponsored Temples")]

During the New Kingdom – the 18th Dynasty especially – it was common for Kings to commission large and elaborate temples dedicated to the major gods of Egypt. These structures, built from limestone or sandstone (materials more permanent than the mud brick used for earlier temples) and filled with rare materials and vibrant wall paintings, exemplify the wealth and access to resources that Egyptian Empire enjoyed during the New Kingdom. The temple at [Karnak](/wiki/Karnak "Karnak"), dedicated to Amun-ra, is one of the largest and best surviving examples of this type of state-sponsored architecture.[[63]](#cite_note-:5-63)

#### Amarna art (c. 1350 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Amarna art (c. 1350 BC)")]

Main article: [Amarna art](/wiki/Amarna_art "Amarna art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Nofretete_Neues_Museum.jpg/250px-Nofretete_Neues_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nofretete_Neues_Museum.jpg)

The *[Bust of Nefertiti](/wiki/Nefertiti_Bust "Nefertiti Bust")*; 1352–1336 BC; limestone, plaster & paint; height: 48 cm; from [Amarna](/wiki/Amarna "Amarna") (Egypt); [Egyptian Museum of Berlin](/wiki/Egyptian_Museum_of_Berlin "Egyptian Museum of Berlin") (Germany)

Amarna art is named for the extensive archeological site at Tel el-[Amarna](/wiki/Amarna "Amarna"), where Pharaoh [Akhenaten](/wiki/Akhenaten "Akhenaten") moved the capital in the late [Eighteenth Dynasty](/wiki/Eighteenth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt"). This period, and the years leading up to it, constitute the most drastic interruption in the style of Egyptian art in the Old, Middle, and New Kingdoms as a result of the rising prominence of the New Solar Theology and the eventual shift towards [Atenism](/wiki/Atenism "Atenism") under Akhenaten.[[67]](#cite_note-67) [Amarna art](/wiki/Amarna_art "Amarna art") is characterized by a sense of movement and a "subjective and sensual perception" of reality as it appeared in the world. Scenes often include overlapping figures creating the sensation of a crowd, which was less common in earlier times.

The artwork produced under Akhenaten was a reflection of the dramatic changes in culture, style, and religion that occurred under Akhenaten's rule. Sometimes called the New Solar Theology, the [new religion](/wiki/Atenism "Atenism") was a [monotheistic](/wiki/Monotheism "Monotheism") worship of the sun, the Aten. Akhenaten emphasized himself as the "co-regent", along with the Aten, as well as the mouthpiece of the Aten himself. Since the sun disk was worshiped as the ultimate life-giving power in this new theology, anything the sun's rays touched was blessed by this force. As a result, sacrifices and worship were likely conducted in open courtyards, and the [sunken relief](/wiki/Sunk_relief "Sunk relief") technique, which works best for outdoor carvings, was also used for indoor works.

[Portrayal of the human body](/wiki/Portraiture_in_ancient_Egypt "Portraiture in ancient Egypt") shifted drastically under the reign of Akhenaten. For instance, many depictions of Akhenaten's body give him distinctly feminine qualities, such as large hips, prominent breasts, and a larger stomach and thighs. Facial representations of Akhenaten, such as in the sandstone [Statue of Akhenaten](/wiki/Colossal_Statues_of_Akhenaten_at_East_Karnak "Colossal Statues of Akhenaten at East Karnak"), display him with an elongated chin, full lips, and hollow cheeks. These stylistic features extended past representations of Akhenaten and were further employed in the depiction of all figures of the royal family, as observed in the Portrait of [Meritaten](/wiki/Meritaten "Meritaten") and Fragment of a queen's face. This is a divergence from the earlier Egyptian art, which emphasized idealized youth and masculinity for male figures.

A notable innovation from the reign of Akhenaten was the religious elevation of the royal family, including Akhenaten's wife, [Nefertiti](/wiki/Nefertiti "Nefertiti"), and their three daughters.[[68]](#cite_note-:3-68) While earlier periods of Egyptian art depicted the king as the primary link between humanity and the gods, the Amarna period extended this power to those of the royal family.[[68]](#cite_note-:3-68) As visualized in the relief of a royal family and the different [talatat](/wiki/Talatat "Talatat") blocks, each figure of the royal family is touched by the rays of the [Aten](/wiki/Aten "Aten"). Nefertiti specifically is believed to have held a significant cultic role during this period.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Not many buildings from this period have survived, partially as they were constructed with standard-sized blocks, known as talatat, which were very easy to remove and reuse. Temples in Amarna, following the trend, did not follow traditional Egyptian customs and were open, without ceilings, and had no closing doors. In the generations after Akhenaten's death, artists reverted to the traditional Egyptian styles of earlier periods. There were still traces of this period's style in later art, but in most respects, Egyptian art, like Egyptian religion, resumed its usual characteristics as though the period had never happened. Amarna itself was abandoned and considerable effort was undertaken to deface monuments from the reign, including disassembling buildings and reusing the blocks with their decoration facing inwards, as has recently been discovered in one later building.[[70]](#cite_note-70) The last King of the Eighteenth Dynasty, [Horemheb](/wiki/Horemheb "Horemheb"), sought to [eliminate](/wiki/Damnatio_memoriae "Damnatio memoriae") the influence of Amarna art and culture and reinstate the tradition powerful of the cult of Amun.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

##### Ramesside Period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Ramesside Period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Ramesses9.jpg/250px-Ramesses9.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ramesses9.jpg)

Portrait of [Ramesses IX](/wiki/Ramesses_IX "Ramesses IX") (ruled 1129–1111 BC), from his tomb [KV6](/wiki/KV6 "KV6"). [20th Dynasty](/wiki/20th_Dynasty "20th Dynasty").

With a concerted effort from Horemheb, the last King of Dynasty Eighteen, to eradicate all Amarna art and influence, the style of the art and architecture of the Empire transitioned into the Ramesside Period for the remainder of the New Kingdom ([Nineteen](/wiki/19th_Dynasty "19th Dynasty") and [Twentieth Dynasties](/wiki/20th_Dynasty "20th Dynasty")).[[64]](#cite_note-:4-64) In response to the religious and artistic revolution of the Amarna period, state-commissioned works demonstrate a clear return to tradition forms and renewed dedication to Amun-ra. However, some elements of Amarna bodily proportion persist; the small of the back does not move back to its lower, Middle Kingdom, height and human limbs remain somewhat elongated. With some modifications, 19th and 20th Dynasty Kings continued to build their funerary temples, which were dedicated to Amun-ra and located in Thebes, in their predecessors' style. The Ramses Kings also continued to build colossal statues such as those commissioned by Hatshepsut.[[63]](#cite_note-:5-63)

During the Ramesside period kings made further contributions to the Temple at Karnak. The Great Hypostyle Hall, commissioned by Sety I (19th Dynasty), consisted of 134 sandstone columns supporting a 20-meter-high ceiling, and covering an acre of land. Sety I decorated most surfaces with intricate bas-relief while his successor, Ramses II added sunken relief work to the walls and columns in the southern side of the Great Hall. The interior carvings show king-god interactions, such as traditional legitimization of power scenes, processions, and rituals. Expansive depictions of military campaigns cover the exterior walls of Hypostyle Hall. Battle scenes illustrating chaotic, disordered enemies strewn over the conquered land and the victorious king as the most prominent figure, trademark the Ramesside period.[[63]](#cite_note-:5-63)

The last period of the New Kingdom demonstrates a return to traditional Egyptian form and style, but the culture is not purely a reversion to the past. The art of the Ramesside period demonstrates the integration of canonized Egypt forms with modern innovations and materials. Advancements such as adorning all surfaces of tombs with paintings and relief and the addition of new funerary texts to burial chambers demonstrate the non-static nature of this period.[[63]](#cite_note-:5-63)

### Third Intermediate Period (c. 1069–664 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Third Intermediate Period (c. 1069–664 BC)")]

Main articles: [Third Intermediate Period of Egypt](/wiki/Third_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "Third Intermediate Period of Egypt") and [Black Pharaohs](/wiki/Black_Pharaohs "Black Pharaohs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Taharqa%2C_Louvre_Museum.jpg/120px-Taharqa%2C_Louvre_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taharqa,_Louvre_Museum.jpg)

[Taharqa](/wiki/Taharqa "Taharqa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Tantamani%2C_Louvre_Museum_reconstruction.jpg/120px-Tantamani%2C_Louvre_Museum_reconstruction.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tantamani,_Louvre_Museum_reconstruction.jpg)

[Tantamani](/wiki/Tantamani "Tantamani")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Senkamanisken_statue_reconstruction%2C_with_Kushite_headress.jpg/120px-Senkamanisken_statue_reconstruction%2C_with_Kushite_headress.jpg)](/wiki/File:Senkamanisken_statue_reconstruction,_with_Kushite_headress.jpg)

[Senkamanisken](/wiki/Senkamanisken "Senkamanisken")

Statues of the "Black Pharaohs" (rulers of the [25th Dynasty](/wiki/25th_Dynasty "25th Dynasty") and the [Kingdom of Kush](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kush "Kingdom of Kush")), in the [Louvre Museum](/wiki/Louvre_Museum "Louvre Museum") (golden colors reconstructed through color pigment analysis)

The Third Intermediate Period was one of decline and political instability, coinciding with the [Late Bronze Age collapse](/wiki/Late_Bronze_Age_collapse "Late Bronze Age collapse") of civilizations in the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East") and [Eastern Mediterranean](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean "Eastern Mediterranean") (including the [Greek Dark Ages](/wiki/Greek_Dark_Ages "Greek Dark Ages")). It was marked by division of the state for much of the period and conquest and rule by foreigners.[[72]](#cite_note-72) After an early period of fracturation, the country was firmly reunited by the [Twenty-second Dynasty](/wiki/Twenty-second_dynasty_of_Egypt "Twenty-second dynasty of Egypt") founded by [Shoshenq I](/wiki/Shoshenq_I "Shoshenq I") in 945 BC (or 943 BC), who descended from [Meshwesh](/wiki/Meshwesh "Meshwesh") immigrants, originally from [Ancient Libya](/wiki/Ancient_Libya "Ancient Libya"). The next period of the [Twenty-fourth Dynasty](/wiki/Twenty-fourth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Twenty-fourth Dynasty of Egypt") saw the increasing influence of the [Nubian](/wiki/Nubian_people "Nubian people") kingdom to the south took full advantage of this division and the ensuing political instability. Then around 732 BC, [Piye](/wiki/Piye "Piye"), marched north and defeated the combined might of several native Egyptian rulers: Peftjaubast, Osorkon IV of Tanis, [Iuput II](/wiki/Iuput_II "Iuput II") of Leontopolis and [Tefnakht](/wiki/Tefnakht "Tefnakht") of Sais. He established the [Twenty-fifth Dynasty](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Twenty-fifth Dynasty of Egypt") of "Black Pharaos" originating from Nubia.

The Third Intermediate Period generally sees a return to archaic Egyptian styles, with particular reference to the art of the [Old](/wiki/Old_Kingdom "Old Kingdom") and [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt").[[73]](#cite_note-AL-73) The art of the period essentially consists in traditional Egyptian styles, reintroduced for unknown reasons as early as [Shoshenq V](/wiki/Shoshenq_V "Shoshenq V") or [Osorkon III](/wiki/Osorkon_III "Osorkon III"),[[74]](#cite_note-74) sometimes with the inclusion of some foreign characteristics, such as the particular iconography of the statues of the Nubian rulers of the [Twenty-fifth Dynasty](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Twenty-fifth Dynasty of Egypt").[[73]](#cite_note-AL-73) Although the Twenty-fifth Dynasty controlled Ancient Egypt for only 73 years, it holds an important place in Egyptian history due to the restoration of traditional Egyptian values, culture, art, and architecture, combined with some original creations such as the [monumental column of Taharqa](/wiki/File:Karnak_R03.jpg "File:Karnak R03.jpg") in [Karnak](/wiki/Karnak "Karnak").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobins2008[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidYD-z8hmdUMICpgPA216_216]-75)[[76]](#cite_note-Taharqa-76) During the 25th dynasty Egypt was ruled from [Napata](/wiki/Napata "Napata") in [Nubia](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia"), now in modern [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan"), and the Dynasty in turn permitted the expansion of Egyptian architectural styles to Lower Egypt and [Nubia](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia").[[73]](#cite_note-AL-73)

### Late Period (c. 664–332 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Late Period (c. 664–332 BC)")]

Main articles: [Late Period of ancient Egypt](/wiki/Late_Period_of_ancient_Egypt "Late Period of ancient Egypt") and [Achaemenid Egypt](/wiki/Achaemenid_Egypt "Achaemenid Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/National_Meusem_Darafsh_6_%2842%29.JPG/250px-National_Meusem_Darafsh_6_%2842%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:National_Meusem_Darafsh_6_(42).JPG)

[Egyptian statue of Achaemenid Emperor Darius I](/wiki/Egyptian_statue_of_Darius_I "Egyptian statue of Darius I"), as Pharaoh of the [Twenty-seventh Dynasty of Egypt](/wiki/Twenty-seventh_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Twenty-seventh Dynasty of Egypt");[[77]](#cite_note-77) 522–486 BC; [greywacke](/wiki/Greywacke "Greywacke"); height: 2.46 m;[[78]](#cite_note-78) [National Museum of Iran](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Iran "National Museum of Iran") ([Teheran](/wiki/Teheran "Teheran"))

In 525 BC, the political state of Egypt was taken over by the Persians, almost a century and a half into Egypt's Late Period. By 404 BC, the Persians were expelled from Egypt, starting a short period of independence. These 60 years of Egyptian rule were marked by an abundance of usurpers and short reigns. The Egyptians were then reoccupied by the Achaemenids until 332 BC with the arrival of [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great"). Sources state that the Egyptians were cheering when Alexander entered the capital since he drove out the immensely disliked Persians. The Late Period is marked with the death of Alexander the Great and the start of the [Ptolemaic dynasty](/wiki/Ptolemaic_dynasty "Ptolemaic dynasty").[[79]](#cite_note-LO-79) Although this period marks political turbulence and immense change for Egypt, its art and culture continued to flourish.

This can be seen in Egyptian temples starting with the [Thirtieth Dynasty](/wiki/Thirtieth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Thirtieth Dynasty of Egypt"), the fifth dynasty in the Late Period, and extending into the Ptolemaic era.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These temples ranged from the Delta to the island of [Philae](/wiki/Philae "Philae").[[79]](#cite_note-LO-79) While Egypt underwent outside influences through trade and conquest by foreign states, these temples remained in the traditional Egyptian style with very little [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic_art "Hellenistic art") influence.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Another relief originating from the Thirtieth Dynasty was the rounded modeling of the body and limbs,[[79]](#cite_note-LO-79) which gave the subjects a more fleshy or heavy effect. For example, for female figures, their breasts would swell and overlap the upper arm in painting. In more realistic portrayals, men would be fat or wrinkled.

Another type of art that became increasingly common during this period was the [Horus stelae](/wiki/Horus "Horus").[[79]](#cite_note-LO-79) These originate from the late New Kingdom and intermediate period but were increasingly common during the fourth century to the Ptolemaic era. These statues would often depict a young Horus holding snakes and standing on some kind of dangerous beast. The depiction of Horus comes from the Egyptian myth where a young Horus is saved from a scorpion bite, resulting in his gaining power over all dangerous animals. These statues were used "to ward off attacks from harmful creatures, and to cure snake bites and scorpion stings".[[79]](#cite_note-LO-79)

### Ptolemaic Period (305–30 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Ptolemaic Period (305–30 BC)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Cleopatra_Isis_Louvre_E27113.jpg/250px-Cleopatra_Isis_Louvre_E27113.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cleopatra_Isis_Louvre_E27113.jpg)

Stele; limestone; 52.4 × 28 × 4 cm; [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre"). A votive inscription dedicated by a [Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") called Onnophris that depicts a male [pharaoh](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh") presenting offerings to the goddess [Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis"), but honours in its text a queen Cleopatra (probably [Cleopatra VII](/wiki/Cleopatra_VII "Cleopatra VII")).[[note 1]](#cite_note-81)

Main article: [Ptolemaic Kingdom](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom")

Discoveries made since the end of the 19th century surrounding the (now submerged) ancient Egyptian city of [Heracleion](/wiki/Heracleion "Heracleion") at [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") include a 4th century BC, unusually sensual, detailed and feministic (as opposed to deified) depiction of [Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis"), marking a combination of Egyptian and Hellenistic forms beginning around the time of Egypt's conquest by [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") in 332–331 BC. However, this was atypical of Ptolemaic sculpture, which generally avoided mixing Egyptian styles with the [Hellenistic style](/wiki/Hellenistic_art "Hellenistic art") used in the court art of the [Ptolemaic dynasty](/wiki/Ptolemaic_dynasty "Ptolemaic dynasty"),[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1991206,_208–209-82) while temples in the rest of the country continued using late versions of traditional Egyptian formulae.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1991210-83) Scholars have proposed an "Alexandrian style" in Hellenistic sculpture, but there is in fact little to connect it with Alexandria.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1991205-84)

[Marble](/wiki/Marble "Marble") was extensively used in court art, although it all had to be imported and use was made of various marble-saving techniques, such as using a number of pieces attached with [stucco](/wiki/Stucco "Stucco"); a head might have the beard, the back of the head and hair in separate pieces.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1991206-85) In contrast to the art of other [Hellenistic kingdoms](/wiki/Hellenistic_kingdoms "Hellenistic kingdoms"), Ptolemaic royal portraits are generalized and idealized, with little concern for achieving an individual portrait, though coins allow some portrait sculpture to be identified as one of the fifteen King Ptolemys.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1991207-86) Many later portraits have clearly had the face reworked to show a later king.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1991209-87) One Egyptian trait was to give much greater prominence to the queens than other successor dynasties to Alexander, with the royal couple often shown as a pair. This predated the 2nd century, when a series of queens exercised real power.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1991208-88)

In the 2nd century, Egyptian temple sculptures began to reuse court models in their faces, and sculptures of a priest often used a Hellenistic style to achieve individually distinctive portrait heads.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1991208–209,_210-89) Many small statuettes were produced, with the most common types being Alexander, a generalized "King Ptolemy", and a naked [Aphrodite](/wiki/Aphrodite "Aphrodite"). Pottery figurines included grotesques and fashionable ladies of the [Tanagra figurine](/wiki/Tanagra_figurine "Tanagra figurine") style.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1991210-83) Erotic groups featured absurdly large phalli. Some fittings for wooden interiors include very delicately patterned polychrome falcons in faience.

### Roman Period (30 BC–619 AD)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Roman Period (30 BC–619 AD)")]

See also: [Egypt (Roman province)](/wiki/Egypt_(Roman_province) "Egypt (Roman province)")

[![Marble staue of a woman holding a rattle in one hand and a pitcher in the other.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Marble_statue_of_Isis%2C_the_goddess_holds_a_situla_and_sistrum%2C_ritual_implements_used_in_her_worship%2C_from_117_until_138_AD%2C_found_at_Hadrian%27s_Villa_%28Pantanello%29%2C_Palazzo_Nuovo%2C_Capitoline_Museums_%2812945630725%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marble_statue_of_Isis,_the_goddess_holds_a_situla_and_sistrum,_ritual_implements_used_in_her_worship,_from_117_until_138_AD,_found_at_Hadrian%27s_Villa_(Pantanello),_Palazzo_Nuovo,_Capitoline_Museums_(12945630725).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Trajan_on_the_Roman_Mammisi_at_Dendera%2C_Egypt.jpg/250px-Trajan_on_the_Roman_Mammisi_at_Dendera%2C_Egypt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trajan_on_the_Roman_Mammisi_at_Dendera,_Egypt.jpg)

Roman Emperor [Trajan](/wiki/Trajan "Trajan") (ruled 98–117 AD) making offerings to Egyptian Gods, on the Roman [Mammisi](/wiki/Mammisi "Mammisi") at the [Dendera Temple complex](/wiki/Dendera_Temple_complex "Dendera Temple complex"), Egypt.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

The [Fayum mummy portraits](/wiki/Fayum_mummy_portraits "Fayum mummy portraits") are probably the most famous example of Egyptian art during the Roman period of Egypt. They were a type of naturalistic painted [portrait](/wiki/Portrait "Portrait") on wooden boards attached to [upper class](/wiki/Upper_Class "Upper Class") [mummies](/wiki/Mummy "Mummy") from [Roman Egypt](/wiki/Roman_Egypt "Roman Egypt"). They belong to the tradition of [panel painting](/wiki/Panel_painting "Panel painting"), one of the most highly regarded forms of art in the [Classical world](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"). The Fayum portraits are the only large body of art from that tradition to have survived.

Mummy portraits have been found across [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), but are most common in the [Faiyum Basin](/wiki/Faiyum_Oasis "Faiyum Oasis"), particularly from [Hawara](/wiki/Hawara "Hawara") (hence the common name) and the [Hadrianic](/wiki/Hadrian "Hadrian") [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") city [Antinoopolis](/wiki/Antinoopolis "Antinoopolis"). "Faiyum portraits" is generally used as a stylistic, rather than a geographic, description. While painted [cartonnage](/wiki/Cartonnage "Cartonnage") mummy cases date back to pharaonic times, the Faiyum mummy portraits were an innovation dating to the time of the [Roman occupation of Egypt](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire").[[91]](#cite_note-92)

The portraits date to the [Imperial Roman era](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), from the late 1st century BC or the early 1st century AD onwards. It is not clear when their production ended, but recent research suggests the middle of the 3rd century. They are among the largest groups among the very few survivors of the [panel painting](/wiki/Panel_painting "Panel painting") tradition of the classical world, which was continued into [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_art "Byzantine art") and Western traditions in the post-classical world, including the local tradition of [Coptic iconography](/wiki/Coptic_art "Coptic art") in Egypt.

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

Egyptian art is known for its distinctive figure convention used for the main figures in both [relief](/wiki/Relief "Relief") and painting, with parted legs (where not seated) and head shown as seen from the side, but the torso seen as from the front. The figures also have a standard set of proportions, measuring 18 "fists" from the ground to the hair-line on the forehead.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithSimpson199833-94) This appears as early as the [Narmer Palette](/wiki/Narmer_Palette "Narmer Palette") from Dynasty I, but this idealized figure convention is not employed in the use of displaying minor figures shown engaged in some activity, such as captives and corpses.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithSimpson199812–13_and_note_17-95) Other conventions make statues of males darker than those of females. Very conventionalized portrait statues appear from as early as the Second Dynasty (before 2,780 BC),[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithSimpson199821–24-96) and with the exception of the [art of the Amarna period](/wiki/Amarna_art "Amarna art") of [Ahkenaten](/wiki/Ahkenaten "Ahkenaten")[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithSimpson1998170–178,_192–194-97) and some other periods such as the Twelfth Dynasty, the idealized features of rulers, like other Egyptian artistic conventions, changed little until the Greek conquest.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithSimpson1998102–103,_133–134-98) Egyptian art uses [hierarchical proportions](/wiki/Hierarchical_proportion "Hierarchical proportion"), where the size of figures indicates their relative importance. The gods or the divine [pharaoh](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh") are usually larger than other figures while the figures of high officials or the tomb owner are usually smaller, and at the smallest scale are any servants, entertainers, animals, trees, and architectural details.[[98]](#cite_note-99)

### Anonymity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Anonymity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Craftsmen%2C_Tomb_of_Nebamun_and_Ipuky_MET_DT10888.jpg/500px-Craftsmen%2C_Tomb_of_Nebamun_and_Ipuky_MET_DT10888.jpg)](/wiki/File:Craftsmen,_Tomb_of_Nebamun_and_Ipuky_MET_DT10888.jpg)

Depiction of craftworkers in ancient Egypt

Ancient Egyptian artists rarely left their names. The Egyptian artwork is anonymous also because most of the time it was collective. [Diodorus of Sicily](/wiki/Diodorus_Siculus "Diodorus Siculus"), who traveled and lived in Egypt, has written: "So, after the craftsmen have decided the height of the statue, they all go home to make the parts which they have chosen" (I, 98).[[99]](#cite_note-100)

### Symbolism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Symbolism")]

Symbolism pervaded Egyptian art and played an important role in establishing a sense of order. The pharaoh's regalia, for example, represented his power to maintain order. Animals were also highly symbolic figures in Egyptian art. Some colors were expressive.[[100]](#cite_note-:1-101)

The [ancient Egyptian language](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_language "Ancient Egyptian language") had four basic color terms: *kem* (black), *hedj* (white/silver), *wadj* (green/blue) and *desher* (red/orange/yellow). Blue, for example, symbolized fertility, birth, and the life-giving waters of the Nile.[[101]](#cite_note-102)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Blue and green were the colors of vegetation, and hence of rejuvenation. [Osiris](/wiki/Osiris "Osiris") could be shown with green skin; in the [26th Dynasty](/wiki/26th_Dynasty "26th Dynasty"), the faces of coffins were often colored green to assist in rebirth.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson200855-103)

This color symbolism explains the popularity of [turquoise](/wiki/Turquoise "Turquoise") and [faience](/wiki/Faience "Faience") in funerary equipment. The use of black for royal figures similarly expressed the fertile alluvial soil[[100]](#cite_note-:1-101) of the Nile from which Egypt was born, and carried connotations of fertility and regeneration. Hence statues of the king as [Osiris](/wiki/Osiris "Osiris") often showed him with black skin. Black was also associated with the afterlife, and was the color of funerary deities such as [Anubis](/wiki/Anubis "Anubis").

Gold indicated divinity due to its unnatural appearance and association with precious materials.[[100]](#cite_note-:1-101) Furthermore, gold was regarded by the ancient Egyptians as "the flesh of the god".[[103]](#cite_note-:2-104) Silver, referred to as "white gold" by the Egyptians, was likewise called "the bones of the god".[[103]](#cite_note-:2-104)

Red, orange and yellow were ambivalent colors. They were, naturally, associated with the sun; red stones such as [quartzite](/wiki/Quartzite "Quartzite") were favored for royal statues which stressed the solar aspects of kingship. [Carnelian](/wiki/Carnelian "Carnelian") has similar symbolic associations in jewelry. Red ink was used to write important names on [papyrus](/wiki/Papyrus "Papyrus") documents. Red was also the color of the deserts, and hence associated with [Set](/wiki/Set_(deity) "Set (deity)").

## Materials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Materials")]

### Faience

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Faience")]

Main article: [Egyptian faience](/wiki/Egyptian_faience "Egyptian faience")

Egyptian faience is a ceramic material, made of quartz sand (or crushed quartz), small amounts of lime, and plant ash or natron. The ingredients were mixed together, glazed and fired to a hard shiny finish. Faience was widely used from the Predynastic Period until Islamic times for inlays and small objects, especially [ushabtis](/wiki/Ushabti "Ushabti"). More accurately termed 'glazed composition', Egyptian faience was so named by early Egyptologists after its superficial resemblance to the tin-glazed earthenwares of medieval Italy (originally produced at [Faenza](/wiki/Faenza "Faenza")). The Egyptian word for it was *tjehenet*, which means 'dazzling', and it was probably used, above all, as a cheap substitute for more precious materials like [turquoise](/wiki/Turquoise "Turquoise") and [lapis lazuli](/wiki/Lapis_lazuli "Lapis lazuli"). Indeed, faience was most commonly produced in shades of blue-green, although a large range of colours was possible.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson200878-105)

### Glass

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Glass")]

Although the glassy materials faience and [Egyptian blue](/wiki/Egyptian_blue "Egyptian blue") were manufactured in Egypt from an early period, the technology for making glass itself was only perfected in the early [18th Dynasty](/wiki/18th_Dynasty_of_Egypt "18th Dynasty of Egypt"). It was probably imported from [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"), since the Egyptian words for glass are of foreign origin. The funerary objects of [Amenhotep II](/wiki/Amenhotep_II "Amenhotep II") included many glass artefacts, demonstrating a range of different techniques. At this period, the material was costly and rare, and may have been a royal monopoly. However, by the end of the 18th Dynasty, Egypt probably made sufficient quantities to export glass to other parts of the Eastern Mediterranean. Glass workshops have been excavated at [Amarna](/wiki/Amarna "Amarna") and [Pi-Ramesses](/wiki/Pi-Ramesses "Pi-Ramesses"). The raw materials – [silica](/wiki/Silica "Silica"), [alkali](/wiki/Alkali "Alkali") and lime – were readily available in Egypt, although ready-made ingots of blue glass were also imported from the Levant and have been found in the cargo of the Uluburun shipwreck off the southern coast of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey").[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson200892-106)

### Egyptian blue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Egyptian blue")]

Main article: [Egyptian blue](/wiki/Egyptian_blue "Egyptian blue")

Egyptian blue is a material related to, but distinct from, faience and glass. Also called "frit", Egyptian blue was made from [quartz](/wiki/Quartz "Quartz"), [alkali](/wiki/Alkali "Alkali"), lime and one or more coloring agents (usually [copper compounds](/wiki/Cuprous "Cuprous")). These were heated together until they fused to become a crystalline mass of uniform color (unlike faience in which the core and the surface layer are of different colors). Egyptian blue could be worked by hand or pressed into molds, to make statuettes and other small objects. It could also be ground to produce pigment. It is first attested in the [Fourth Dynasty](/wiki/Fourth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Fourth Dynasty of Egypt"), but became particularly popular in the [Ptolemaic period](/wiki/Ptolemaic_period "Ptolemaic period") and the [Roman period](/wiki/Egypt_(Roman_province) "Egypt (Roman province)"), when it was known as *caeruleum*.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson200873_&_74-107)

The color blue was used only sparingly even up until as late as Dynasty IV, where the color was found adorning mat-patterns in the Tomb of Saccara, which was constructed during the first Dynasty. Until this discovery was made, the color blue had not been known in Egyptian art.[[107]](#cite_note-108)

### Metals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Metals")]

While not a leading center of metallurgy, ancient Egypt nevertheless developed technologies for extracting and processing the metals found within its borders and in neighbouring lands.

Copper was the first metal to be exploited in Egypt. Small beads have been found in [Badarian](/wiki/Badarian_culture "Badarian culture") graves; larger items were produced in the later [Predynastic Period](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt"), by a combination of mould-casting, annealing and cold-hammering. The production of copper artifacts peaked in the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Old Kingdom of Egypt") when huge numbers of copper chisels were manufactured to cut the stone blocks of pyramids. The copper statues of [Pepi I](/wiki/Pepi_I "Pepi I") and [Merenre](/wiki/Merenre_Nemtyemsaf_I "Merenre Nemtyemsaf I") from [Hierakonpolis](/wiki/Hierakonpolis "Hierakonpolis") are rare survivors of large-scale metalworking.

The golden treasure of [Tutankhamun](/wiki/Tutankhamun "Tutankhamun") has come to symbolize the wealth of [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), and illustrates the importance of gold in pharaonic culture. The burial chamber in a royal tomb was called "the house of gold". According to the Egyptian religion, the flesh of the gods was made of gold. A shining metal that never tarnished, it was the ideal material for cult images of deities, for royal funerary equipment, and to add brilliance to the tops of [obelisks](/wiki/Obelisks "Obelisks"). It was used extensively for jewelry, and was distributed to officials as a reward for loyal services ("the gold of honour").

Silver had to be imported from the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"), and its rarity initially gave it greater value than gold (which, like [electrum](/wiki/Electrum "Electrum"), was readily available within the borders of Egypt and Nubia). Early examples of silverwork include the bracelets of the [Hetepheres](/wiki/Hetepheres "Hetepheres"). By the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"), silver seems to have become less valuable than gold, perhaps because of increased trade with the Middle East. The treasure from [El-Tod](/wiki/El-Tod "El-Tod") consisted of a hoard of silver objects, probably made in the Aegean, while silver jewelry made for female members of the [12th Dynasty](/wiki/Twelfth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt") royal family was found at [Dahshur](/wiki/Dahshur "Dahshur") and [Lahun](/wiki/Lahun "Lahun"). In the Egyptian religion, the bones of the gods were said to be made of silver.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson2008153_&_154-109)

Iron was the last metal to be exploited on a large scale by the Egyptians. Meteoritic iron was used for the manufacture of beads from the Badarian period. However, the advanced technology required to smelt iron was not introduced into Egypt until the Late Period. Before that, iron objects were imported and were consequently highly valued for their rarity. The [Amarna letters](/wiki/Amarna_letters "Amarna letters") refer to diplomatic gifts of iron being sent by Near Eastern rulers, especially the [Hittites](/wiki/Hittites "Hittites"), to [Amenhotep III](/wiki/Amenhotep_III "Amenhotep III") and [Akhenaten](/wiki/Akhenaten "Akhenaten"). Iron tools and weapons only became common in Egypt in the [Roman Period](/wiki/Egypt_(Roman_province) "Egypt (Roman province)").

### Wood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Wood")]

Because of its relatively poor survival in archaeological contexts, wood is not particularly well represented among artifacts from Ancient Egypt. Nevertheless, woodworking was evidently carried out to a high standard from an early period. Native trees included [date palm](/wiki/Date_palm "Date palm") and [dom palm](/wiki/Hyphaene_thebaica "Hyphaene thebaica"), the trunks of which could be used as joists in buildings, or split to produce planks. [Tamarisk](/wiki/Tamarisk "Tamarisk"), [acacia](/wiki/Acacia "Acacia") and [sycamore](/wiki/Sycamore "Sycamore") fig were employed in furniture manufacture, while ash was used when greater flexibility was required (for example in the manufacture of bowls). However, all these native timbers were of relatively poor quality; finer varieties had to be imported, especially from the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant").[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson2008263-110)

### Lapis lazuli

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Lapis lazuli")]

See also: [Lapis lazuli](/wiki/Lapis_lazuli "Lapis lazuli")

[Lapis lazuli](/wiki/Lapis_lazuli "Lapis lazuli") is a dark blue semi-precious stone highly valued by the ancient Egyptians because of its symbolic association with the heavens. It was imported via long-distance trade routes from the mountains of north-eastern [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") [[110]](#cite_note-111), and was considered superior to all other materials except gold and silver. Coloured glass or faience provided a cheap imitation. Lapis lazuli is first attested in the [Predynastic Period](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt"). A temporary interruption in supply during the [Second](/wiki/Second_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Second Dynasty of Egypt") and [Third Dynasties](/wiki/Third_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Third Dynasty of Egypt") probably reflects political changes in the [ancient Near East](/wiki/Ancient_Near_East "Ancient Near East"). Thereafter, it was used extensively for jewelry, small figurines and amulets.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson2008134_&_135-112)

### Other materials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Other materials")]

* [Jasper](/wiki/Jasper "Jasper") is an impure form of [chalcedony](/wiki/Chalcedony "Chalcedony") with bands or patches of red, green or yellow. Red jasper, symbol of life and of positive aspects of the universe, was used above all to make amulets. It was ideal for certain amulets, such as the *tit* amulet, or *[tyet](/wiki/Tyet "Tyet")* (also known as *knot of [Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis")*), to be made of red jasper, as specified in Spell 156 of the *[Book of the Dead](/wiki/Book_of_the_Dead "Book of the Dead")*. The more rarely used green jasper was especially indicated for making [scarabs](/wiki/Scarab_(artifact) "Scarab (artifact)"), particularly heart scarabs.
* [Serpentine](/wiki/Serpentine_subgroup "Serpentine subgroup") is the generic term for the hydrated silicates of magnesium. It came mostly from the eastern desert, and occurs in many shades of color, from a pale green to a dark verging on black. Used from the earliest times, it was sought specially for making heart scarabs.
* [Steatite](/wiki/Steatite "Steatite") (also known as soapstone) is a mineral of the chlorite family; it has the great advantage of being very easy to work. Steatite amulets are found in contexts from the [Predynastic Period](/wiki/Predynastic_Period "Predynastic Period") on, although in subsequent periods it was usually covered in a fine layer of faience and was used in the manufacture of numerous scarabs.
* [Turquoise](/wiki/Turquoise "Turquoise") is an opaque stone, sky blue to blue-green. It is a natural aluminium phosphate colored blue by traces of [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"). Closely linked to the goddess [Hathor](/wiki/Hathor "Hathor"), it was extracted mainly from mines in [Sinai](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") (at [Serabit el-Khadim](/wiki/Serabit_el-Khadim "Serabit el-Khadim")). The Egyptians were particularly fond of the greenish shades, symbolic of dynamism and vital renewal. In the [Late Period](/wiki/Late_Period_of_Egypt "Late Period of Egypt"), turquoise (like lapis lazuli) was synonymous with joy and delight.

## Sculpture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Sculpture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/King_Menkaura_%28Mycerinus%29_and_queen.jpg/250px-King_Menkaura_%28Mycerinus%29_and_queen.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Menkaura_(Mycerinus)_and_queen.jpg)

Pharaoh [Menkaura](/wiki/Menkaura "Menkaura") and queen Khamerernebty II; 2490–2472 BC; [greywacke](/wiki/Greywacke "Greywacke"); overall: 142.2 cm × 57.1 cm × 55.2 cm (56.0 in × 22.5 in × 21.7 in); [Museum of Fine Arts, Boston](/wiki/Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston "Museum of Fine Arts, Boston") (US)

The [monumental sculpture](/wiki/Monumental_sculpture "Monumental sculpture") of [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt")'s temples and tombs is well known,[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithSimpson19982-113) but refined and delicate small works exist in much greater numbers. The Egyptians used the technique of [sunk relief](/wiki/Sunk_relief "Sunk relief"), which is best viewed in sunlight for the outlines and forms to be emphasized by shadows. The distinctive pose of standing statues facing forward with one foot in front of the other was helpful for the balance and strength of the piece. This singular pose was used early in the history of Egyptian art and well into the Ptolemaic period, although seated statues were common as well.

Egyptian [pharaohs](/wiki/Pharaohs "Pharaohs") were always regarded as gods, but other deities are much less common in large statues, except when they represent the pharaoh *as* another deity; however, the other deities are frequently shown in paintings and reliefs. The famous row of four colossal statues outside the [main temple at Abu Simbel](/wiki/Abu_Simbel_temples "Abu Simbel temples") each show [Rameses II](/wiki/Rameses_II "Rameses II"), a typical scheme, though here exceptionally large.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithSimpson19984–5,_208–209-114) Most larger sculptures survived from [Egyptian temples](/wiki/Egyptian_temple "Egyptian temple") or tombs; massive statues were built to represent gods and pharaohs and their queens, usually for open areas in or outside temples. The very early colossal [Great Sphinx of Giza](/wiki/Great_Sphinx_of_Giza "Great Sphinx of Giza") was never repeated, but avenues lined with very large statues including [sphinxes](/wiki/Sphinx "Sphinx") and other animals formed part of many temple complexes. The most sacred cult image of a god in a temple, usually held in the [naos](/wiki/Naos_(hieroglyph) "Naos (hieroglyph)"), was in the form of a relatively small boat or [barque](/wiki/Barque "Barque") holding an image of the god, and apparently usually in precious metal – none of these are known to have survived.

By Dynasty IV (2680–2565 BC), the idea of the [Ka statue](/wiki/Ka_statue "Ka statue") was firmly established. These were put in tombs as a resting place for the [*ka* portion of the soul](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_concept_of_the_soul "Ancient Egyptian concept of the soul"), and so there is a good number of less conventionalized statues of well-off administrators and their wives, many in wood as Egypt is one of the few places in the world where the climate allows wood to survive over millennia, and many [block statues](/wiki/Block_statue "Block statue"). The so-called [reserve heads](/wiki/Reserve_head "Reserve head"), plain hairless heads, are especially naturalistic, though the extent to which there was real [portraiture in ancient Egypt](/wiki/Portraiture_in_ancient_Egypt "Portraiture in ancient Egypt") is still debated.

Early tombs also contained small models of the slaves, animals, buildings and objects such as boats (and later *[ushabti](/wiki/Ushabti "Ushabti")* figures) necessary for the deceased to continue his lifestyle in the afterlife.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithSimpson199889–90-115) However, the great majority of wooden sculpture have been lost to decay, or probably used as fuel. Small figures of deities, or their animal personifications, are very common, and found in popular materials such as pottery. There were also large numbers of small carved objects, from figures of the gods to toys and carved utensils. [Alabaster](/wiki/Alabaster "Alabaster") was used for expensive versions of these, though painted wood was the most common material, and was normal for the small models of animals, slaves and possessions placed in tombs to provide for the afterlife.

Very strict conventions were followed while crafting statues, and specific rules governed the appearance of every Egyptian god. For example, the sky god ([Horus](/wiki/Horus "Horus")) was to be represented with a falcon's head, the god of funeral rites ([Anubis](/wiki/Anubis "Anubis")) was to be shown with a jackal's head. Artistic works were ranked according to their compliance with these conventions, and the conventions were followed so strictly that, over three thousand years, the appearance of statues changed very little. These conventions were intended to convey the timeless and non-ageing quality of the figure's *ka*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A common relief in ancient Egyptian sculpture was the difference between the representation of men and women. Women were often represented in an idealistic form, young and pretty, and rarely shown in an older maturity. Men were shown in either an idealistic manner or a more realistic depiction.[[62]](#cite_note-:0-62) Sculptures of men often showed men that aged, since the regeneration of ageing was a positive thing for them whereas women are shown as perpetually young.[[115]](#cite_note-116)

* [![Both sides of the Narmer Palette; c. 3100 BC; greywacke; height: 63 cm; from Hierakonpolis (Egypt); Egyptian Museum (Cairo).
