### Stele

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Stele")]

Main article: [Stele](/wiki/Stele "Stele")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Princess_Nefertiabet_before_her_meal-E_15591-IMG_9645-gradient.jpg/250px-Princess_Nefertiabet_before_her_meal-E_15591-IMG_9645-gradient.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princess_Nefertiabet_before_her_meal-E_15591-IMG_9645-gradient.jpg)

Stele of Princess [Nefertiabet](/wiki/Nefertiabet "Nefertiabet") eating; 2589–2566 BC; limestone & paint; 37.7 × 52.5 × 8.3 cm; from [Giza](/wiki/Giza "Giza"); [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre")

A stele is an upright tablet of stone or wood, often with a curved top, painted and carved with text and pictures. Numerous examples were produced throughout Egyptian history for a variety of purposes, including funerary, votive and commemorative. Funerary stelae, attested from the early [1st Dynasty](/wiki/First_Dynasty_of_Egypt "First Dynasty of Egypt"), typically bore the name and titles of the deceased. This basic form, which served to identify the tomb owner, evolved into a key component of the funerary equipment with a magical function. Hence, from the [2nd Dynasty](/wiki/Second_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Second Dynasty of Egypt") onward, the owner was usually shown seated before an offering table piled with food and drink; in the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"), the offering formula was generally inscribed along the top of the stele. Both were designed to ensure a perpetual supply of offerings in the afterlife. Votive stelae, inscribed with prayers to deities, were dedicated by worshipers seeking a favorable outcome to a particular situation. In the Middle Kingdom, many hundreds were set up by pilgrims on the "terrace of the great god" at [Abydos](/wiki/Abydos,_Egypt "Abydos, Egypt"), so that they might participate in the annual procession of [Osiris](/wiki/Osiris "Osiris"). One particular variety of votive stele common in the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") was the ear stele, inscribed with images of human ears to encourage the deity to listen to the prayer or request.

Commemorative stelae were produced to proclaim notable achievements (for example, the stela of [Horwerra](/w/index.php?title=Horwerra&action=edit&redlink=1 "Horwerra (page does not exist)"), recording a mining expedition to [Serabit el-Khadim](/wiki/Serabit_el-Khadim "Serabit el-Khadim"), and the [Restoration Stela](/wiki/Restoration_Stela "Restoration Stela") of [Tutankhamun](/wiki/Tutankhamun "Tutankhamun"), celebrating the restoration of the traditional cults at the end of the [Amarna period](/wiki/Amarna_period "Amarna period")); to celebrate military victories (for instance, the [Merneptah Stele](/wiki/Merneptah_Stele "Merneptah Stele")); and to establish frontiers (for example the Semna stele of [Senusret III](/wiki/Senusret_III "Senusret III") and the boundary stelae around Amarna).[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson2008235-118)

### Pyramidia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Pyramidia")]

Main article: [Pyramidion](/wiki/Pyramidion "Pyramidion")

A pyramidion is a capstone at the top of a pyramid. Called benbenet in [ancient Egyptian language](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_language "Ancient Egyptian language"), it associated the pyramid as a whole with the sacred [Benben stone](/wiki/Benben#Benben_stone "Benben"). Pyramidia may have been covered in [gold leaf](/wiki/Gold_leaf "Gold leaf") to reflect the rays of the sun; in the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"), they were often inscribed with royal titles and religious symbols.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson2008197-120)

## Painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Painting")]

Main article: [Painting in Ancient Egypt](/wiki/Painting_in_Ancient_Egypt "Painting in Ancient Egypt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Ocas_de_Meidum_%28completo_en_alta_resoluci%C3%B3n%29.jpg/1280px-Ocas_de_Meidum_%28completo_en_alta_resoluci%C3%B3n%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ocas_de_Meidum_(completo_en_alta_resoluci%C3%B3n).jpg "The Meidum geese; 2575–2551 BC; painted gypsum; 27 x 172 cm; Egyptian Museum (Cairo)")

The *Meidum geese*; 2575–2551 BC; painted gypsum; 27 x 172 cm; [Egyptian Museum](/wiki/Egyptian_Museum "Egyptian Museum") ([Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"))

Not all Egyptian [reliefs](/wiki/Relief "Relief") were painted, and less-prestigious works in tombs, temples and palaces were merely painted on a flat surface. Stone surfaces were prepared by whitewash, or if rough, a layer of coarse mud plaster, with a smoother *[gesso](/wiki/Gesso "Gesso")* layer above; some finer [limestones](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") could take paint directly. Pigments were mostly mineral, chosen to withstand strong sunlight without fading. The binding medium used in painting remains unclear: [egg tempera](/wiki/Egg_tempera "Egg tempera") and various gums and resins have been suggested. It is clear that true [fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco"), painted into a thin layer of wet plaster, was not used. Instead, the paint was applied to dried plaster, in what is called *fresco a secco* in Italian. After painting, a varnish or resin was usually applied as a protective coating, and many paintings with some exposure to the elements have survived remarkably well, although those on fully exposed walls rarely have.[[120]](#cite_note-121) Small objects including wooden statuettes were often painted using similar techniques.

Many ancient Egyptian paintings have survived in tombs, and sometimes temples, due to Egypt's extremely dry climate. The paintings were often made with the intent of making a pleasant afterlife for the deceased. The themes included journeys through the afterworld or protective deities introducing the deceased to the gods of the underworld (such as [Osiris](/wiki/Osiris "Osiris")). Some tomb paintings show activities that the deceased were involved in when they were alive and wished to carry on doing for eternity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") period and afterwards, the *[Book of the Dead](/wiki/Book_of_the_Dead "Book of the Dead")* was buried with the entombed person. It was considered important for an introduction to the afterlife.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

Egyptian paintings are painted in such a way to show a side view and a front view of the animal or person at the same time. For example, the painting to the right shows the head from a profile view and the body from a frontal view. Their main colors were red, blue, green, gold, black and yellow.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Paintings showing scenes of hunting and fishing can have lively close-up landscape backgrounds of reeds and water, but in general Egyptian painting did not develop a sense of depth, and neither landscapes nor a sense of visual perspective are found, the figures rather varying in size with their importance rather than their location.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Architecture")]

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian architecture](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_architecture "Ancient Egyptian architecture")

Further information: [Egyptian pyramids](/wiki/Egyptian_pyramids "Egyptian pyramids") and [Egyptian temple](/wiki/Egyptian_temple "Egyptian temple")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Medinet_Habu_2016-03-23g.jpg/250px-Medinet_Habu_2016-03-23g.jpg)](/wiki/File:Medinet_Habu_2016-03-23g.jpg)

Winged sun on a [cavetto](/wiki/Cavetto "Cavetto") from the Medinet Habu temple complex. The winged sun represents a form of the falcon god [Horus](/wiki/Horus "Horus"), son of [Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis"), triumphant over his enemies. The image was also a common protective device over temple entrances. Winged disks are a popular motif in [Egyptian Revival architecture](/wiki/Egyptian_Revival_architecture "Egyptian Revival architecture") and [art](/wiki/Egyptian_Revival_decorative_arts "Egyptian Revival decorative arts")[[122]](#cite_note-123)

Ancient Egyptian architects used sun-dried and kiln-baked bricks, fine sandstone, limestone and granite. Architects carefully planned all their work. The stones had to fit precisely together, since no mud or mortar was used. When creating the pyramids it is unknown how the workers or stones reached the top of them as no records were kept of their construction. When the top of the structure was completed, the artists decorated from the top down, removing ramp sand as they went down. Exterior walls of structures like the pyramids contained only a few small openings. Hieroglyphic and pictorial carvings in brilliant colors were abundantly used to decorate Egyptian structures, including many motifs, like the scarab, sacred beetle, the solar disk, and the vulture. They described the changes the Pharaoh would go through to become a god.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

As early as 2600 BC the architect Imhotep made use of stone columns whose surface was carved to reflect the organic form of bundled reeds, like papyrus, lotus and palm; in later Egyptian architecture faceted cylinders were also common. Their form is thought to derive from archaic reed-built shrines. Carved from stone, the columns were highly decorated with carved and painted hieroglyphs, texts, ritual imagery and natural motifs. One of the most important types are the papyriform columns. The origin of these columns goes back to the [5th Dynasty](/wiki/5th_Dynasty "5th Dynasty"). They are composed of lotus (papyrus) stems which are drawn together into a bundle decorated with bands: the capital, instead of opening out into the shape of a bellflower, swells out and then narrows again like a flower in bud. The base, which tapers to take the shape of a half-sphere like the stem of the lotus, has a continuously recurring decoration of stipules. At the [Luxor Temple](/wiki/Luxor_Temple "Luxor Temple"), the columns are reminiscent of papyrus bundles, perhaps symbolic of the marsh from which the ancient Egyptians believed the creation of the world to have unfolded.

## Jewelry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Jewelry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Bead_in_the_Form_of_a_Lotus_Petal_MET_11.215.369.jpg/60px-Bead_in_the_Form_of_a_Lotus_Petal_MET_11.215.369.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bead_in_the_Form_of_a_Lotus_Petal_MET_11.215.369.jpg)

Various beads from the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") (New York City)

The ancient Egyptians exhibited a love of ornament and personal decoration from earliest [Predynastic](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt") times. [Badarian](/wiki/Badari_culture "Badari culture") burials often contained strings of beads made from glazed [steatite](/wiki/Steatite "Steatite"), shell and [ivory](/wiki/Ivory "Ivory"). Jewelry in gold, silver, copper and [faience](/wiki/Faience "Faience") is also attested in the early [Predynastic period](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt"); more varied materials were introduced in the centuries preceding the [1st Dynasty](/wiki/First_Dynasty_of_Egypt "First Dynasty of Egypt"). By the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom "Old Kingdom"), the combination of [carnelian](/wiki/Carnelian "Carnelian"), [turquoise](/wiki/Turquoise "Turquoise") and [lapis lazuli](/wiki/Lapis_lazuli "Lapis lazuli") had been established for royal jewelry, and this was to become standard in the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"). Less sophisticated pieces might use [bone](/wiki/Bone "Bone"), [mother-of-pearl](/wiki/Mother-of-pearl "Mother-of-pearl") or [cowrie shells](/wiki/Cowrie_shells "Cowrie shells").

The particular choice of materials depended upon practical, aesthetical and symbolic considerations. Some types of jewelry remained perennially popular, while others went in and out of fashion. In the first category were bead necklaces, bracelets, armlets and girdles. Bead aprons are first attested in the 1st Dynasty, while [usekh broad collars](/wiki/Usekh_collar "Usekh collar") became a standard type from the early [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom "Old Kingdom"). In the Middle Kingdom, they had fallen from favor, to be replaced by finger-rings and ear ornaments (rings and plugs). [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") jewelry is generally more elaborate and garish than that of earlier periods, and was influenced by styles from the [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"). Many fine examples were found in the tomb of [Tutankhamun](/wiki/Tutankhamun "Tutankhamun"). Jewelry, both royal and private, was replete with religious symbolism. It was also used to display the wealth and rank of the wearer. Royal jewels were always the most elaborate, as exemplified by the pieces found at [Dahshur](/wiki/Dahshur "Dahshur") and [Lahun](/wiki/Lahun "Lahun"), made for princesses of the [18th Dynasty](/wiki/18th_Dynasty_of_Egypt "18th Dynasty of Egypt"), favored courtiers were rewarded with the "gold of honor" as a sign of royal favor.

The techniques of jewelry-making can be reconstructed from surviving artifacts and from tomb decoration. A jeweler's workshop is shown in the tomb of [Mereruka](/wiki/Mereruka "Mereruka"); several [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") tombs at [Thebes](/wiki/Thebes,_Egypt "Thebes, Egypt") contain similar scenes.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson2008119_&_120-125)

## Amulets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Amulets")]

Main article: [List of ancient Egyptian statuary with amulet necklaces](/wiki/List_of_ancient_Egyptian_statuary_with_amulet_necklaces "List of ancient Egyptian statuary with amulet necklaces")

An amulet is a small charm worn to afford its owner magical protection, or to convey certain qualities (for example, a lion amulet might convey strength, or a set-square amulet might convey rectitude). Attested from the [Badarian](/wiki/Badarian_culture "Badarian culture") period onward, amulets were produced both for the living and the dead. Particular amulets were placed at specific places in the mummy wrappings. The [heart scarab](/wiki/Heart_scarab "Heart scarab") was a specialized form of amulet to protect the heart of the deceased in the afterlife. Amulets were made from a wide variety of materials, including [faience](/wiki/Faience "Faience"), glass, and [precious stones](/wiki/Precious_stones "Precious stones") – with color often playing an important symbolic role – and in a wide variety of forms. They might depict sacred objects (such as the [Djed](/wiki/Djed "Djed") pillar, [Tyet](/wiki/Tyet "Tyet") girdle or [Wedjad](/wiki/Eye_of_Horus "Eye of Horus") eye); animals (bull's head amulets were particularly common in the late [Predynastic period](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt")); or [hieroglyphs](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs") (for example, [Ankh](/wiki/Ankh "Ankh") or [Sa](/wiki/Egyptian_biliteral_signs "Egyptian biliteral signs")). From the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") onward, deities – especially household deities such as [Bes](/wiki/Bes "Bes") and [Taweret](/wiki/Taweret "Taweret") – were popular subjects for amulets.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson200824_&_25-126)

### Scarab-shaped amulets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Scarab-shaped amulets")]

Main article: [Scarab (artifact)](/wiki/Scarab_(artifact) "Scarab (artifact)")

The protective amulet for the heart was in the form of the [scarab beetle](/wiki/Scarabaeus_sacer "Scarabaeus sacer"), the manifestation of the creator and solar deity [Khepri](/wiki/Khepri "Khepri"). It was a symbol of new life and resurrection. The scarab beetle was seen to push a ball of dung along the ground, and from this came the idea of the beetle rolling the sun across the sky. Subsequently, the young beetles were observed to hatch from their eggs inside the ball, hence the idea of creation: life springs forth from primordial mud.

The heart scarab was a large scarab amulet which was wrapped in the mummy bandaging over the deceased's heart. It was made from a range of green and dark-colored materials, including faience, glass, glazed [steatite](/wiki/Steatite "Steatite"), [schist](/wiki/Schist "Schist"), [feldspar](/wiki/Feldspar "Feldspar"), [hematite](/wiki/Hematite "Hematite") and obsidian.[[126]](#cite_note-127) Black was also associated with the afterlife, while blue and green were associated with the birth and the life-giving waters of the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile").

## Pottery

Main article: [Ancient Egyptian pottery](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_pottery "Ancient Egyptian pottery")

See also: [Funerary cone](/wiki/Funerary_cone "Funerary cone")

Different types of pottery items were deposited in tombs of the dead. Some such pottery items represented interior parts of the body, such as the [lungs](/wiki/Lung "Lung"), the [liver](/wiki/Liver "Liver") and smaller [intestines](/wiki/Intestine "Intestine"), which were removed before [embalming](/wiki/Embalming "Embalming"). A large number of smaller objects in enamel pottery were also deposited with the dead. It was customary for the tomb walls to be crafted with cones of pottery, about 15 to 25 cm (6 to 10 in) tall, on which were engraved or impressed legends relating to the dead occupants of the tombs. These cones usually contained the names of the deceased, their titles, offices which they held, and some expressions appropriate to funeral purposes.

Main article: [Egyptian hieroglyphs](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs")

Egyptian writing remained a remarkably conservative [system](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system"), and the preserve of a tiny literate minority, while the spoken [language](/wiki/Egyptian_language "Egyptian language") underwent considerable change. Egyptian stelas are decorated with finely carved hieroglyphs.

The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from [proto-literate](/wiki/Proto-writing "Proto-writing") symbol systems in the [Early Bronze Age](/wiki/Early_Bronze_Age "Early Bronze Age"), around the 32nd century BC ([Naqada III](/wiki/Naqada_III "Naqada III")), with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the [Second Dynasty](/wiki/Second_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Second Dynasty of Egypt") (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the [classical language](/wiki/Middle_Egyptian "Middle Egyptian") of the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt") period; during this period, the system made use of about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") and [Late Period](/wiki/Late_Period_of_ancient_Egypt "Late Period of ancient Egypt"), and on into the [Persian](/wiki/Twenty-seventh_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Twenty-seventh Dynasty of Egypt") and [Ptolemaic](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom") periods. Late use of hieroglyphics are found in the [Roman period](/wiki/Egypt_(Roman_province) "Egypt (Roman province)"), extending into the 4th century AD.

## Furniture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Furniture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Quattro_sedie_reali_coi_loro_suppedanei_%28NYPL_b14291206-425431%29.jpg/250px-Quattro_sedie_reali_coi_loro_suppedanei_%28NYPL_b14291206-425431%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quattro_sedie_reali_coi_loro_suppedanei_(NYPL_b14291206-425431).jpg)

Illustrations of four royal chairs with their supporters

Although, by modern standards, ancient Egyptian houses would have been very sparsely furnished, woodworking and cabinet-making were highly developed crafts. All the main types of furniture are attested, either as surviving examples or in tomb decoration. Chairs were only for the wealthy; most people would have used low stools. Beds consisted of a wooden frame, with matting or leather webbing to provide support; the most elaborate beds also had a canopy, hung with netting, to provide extra privacy and protection from insects. The feet of chairs, stools and beds were often modeled to resemble bull hooves or, in later periods, lion feet or duck heads. Wooden furniture was often coated with a layer of [plaster](/wiki/Plaster "Plaster") and painted.

Royal furniture was more elaborate, making use of inlays, veneers and marquetry. Funerary objects from the [tomb of Tutankhamun](/wiki/KV62 "KV62") include tables, boxes and chests, a gilded throne, and ritual beds shaped like elongated hippos and cattle. The burial equipment of [Hetepheres](/wiki/Hetepheres "Hetepheres") included a set of travelling furniture, light and easy to dismantle. Such furniture must have been used on military campaigns and other royal journeys.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson200887-128) Egyptian furniture has highly influenced the development of [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Greco-Roman_world#Culture "Greco-Roman world") furniture. It also was one of the principal sources of inspiration of a style known as [Empire](/wiki/Empire_style "Empire style").[[128]](#cite_note-129) The main [motifs](/wiki/Motif_(visual_arts) "Motif (visual arts)") used are: palm and lotus leaves, flowers, lion heads and claws, bull hooves, bird heads, and geometric combinations. Everything is sober and with a monumental character.[[129]](#cite_note-130)

## Clothing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Clothing")]

Main article: [Clothing in ancient Egypt](/wiki/Clothing_in_ancient_Egypt "Clothing in ancient Egypt")

Artistic representations, supplemented by surviving garments, are the main sources of evidence for ancient Egyptian fashion. The two sources are not always in agreement, however, and it seems that representations were more concerned with highlighting certain attributes of the person depicted than with accurately recordings their true appearance. For example, women were often shown with restrictive, tight-fitting dresses, perhaps to emphasize their figures.

As in most societies, fashions in Egypt changed over time; different clothes were worn in different seasons of the year, and by different sections of society. Particular office-holders, especially priests and the king, had their own special garments.

For the general population, clothing was simple, predominantly of linen, and probably white or off-white in color. It would have shown the dirt easily, and professional launderers are known to have been attached to the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") workmen's village at [Deir el-Medina](/wiki/Deir_el-Medina "Deir el-Medina"). Men would have worn a simple loin-cloth or short kilt (known as *[shendyt](/wiki/Shendyt "Shendyt")*), supplemented in winter by a heavier tunic. High-status individuals could express their status through their clothing, and were more susceptible to changes in fashion.

Longer, more voluminous clothing made an appearance in the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"); flowing, elaborately pleated, diaphanous robes for men and women were particularly popular in the late [18th Dynasty](/wiki/Eighteenth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt") and the Ramesside period. Decorated textiles also became more common in the New Kingdom. In all periods, women's dresses may have been enhanced by colorful bead netting worn over the top. In the [Roman Period](/wiki/Egypt_(Roman_province) "Egypt (Roman province)"), Egypt became known for the manufacture of fine clothing. [Coiled sewn sandals](/wiki/Coiled_sewn_sandals "Coiled sewn sandals") or sandals of leather are the most commonly attested types of footwear. Examples of these, together with linen shirts and other clothing, were discovered in [the tomb of Tutankhamun](/wiki/KV62 "KV62").[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson200853-131)

## Cosmetics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Cosmetics")]

Main article: [Beauty and cosmetics in ancient Egypt](/wiki/Beauty_and_cosmetics_in_ancient_Egypt "Beauty and cosmetics in ancient Egypt")

Use of makeup, especially around the eyes, was a characteristic feature of ancient Egyptian culture from [Predynastic times](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt"). Black [kohl](/wiki/Kohl_(cosmetics) "Kohl (cosmetics)") (eye-paint) was applied to protect the eyes, as well as for aesthetic reasons. It was usually made of [galena](/wiki/Galena "Galena"), giving a silvery-black color; during the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Old Kingdom of Egypt"), green eye-paint was also used, made from [malachite](/wiki/Malachite "Malachite"). Egyptian women painted their lips and cheeks, using rouge made from red ochre. [Henna](/wiki/Henna "Henna") was applied as a dye for hair, fingernails and toenails, and perhaps also nipples. Creams and unguents to condition the skin were popular, and were made from various plant extracts.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson200857-132)

## Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Music of Egypt](/wiki/Music_of_Egypt "Music of Egypt")

On secular and religious occasions, music played an important part in celebrations. Musicians, playing instruments such as the castanets and flute, are depicted on objects from the [Predynastic Period](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt"). A wide range of percussions, wind and string instruments were known to the ancient Egyptian. They include rattles, clappers, drums, tambourines and the [sistrum](/wiki/Sistrum "Sistrum"); pipes, flutes and trumpets; and harps (particularly popular at feasts). The lyre and lute were introduced from the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"). Musical notation is not attested until the early [Ptolemaic Period](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Period "Ptolemaic Period"). Groups of musicians, either mixed gender or female only, are known from the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Old Kingdom of Egypt"). Women singers and [sistrum](/wiki/Sistrum "Sistrum")-players had an important role in temple cults, especially those of [Hathor](/wiki/Hathor "Hathor") and [Isis](/wiki/Isis "Isis"). Tomb decoration from all periods indicates that, as today, groups of workers sang to generate a sense of solidarity and to maintain their enthusiasm.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson2008161-133)

### Sistrum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Sistrum")]

Main article: [Sistrum](/wiki/Sistrum "Sistrum")

A sistrum (plural: sistra) is a rattle used in religious ceremonies, especially temple rituals, and usually played by women. Called a "seshsehet" in Egyptian, the name imitates the swishing sound the small metal disks made when the instrument was shaken. It was closely associated with [Hathor](/wiki/Hathor "Hathor") in her role as "lady of music", and the handle was often decorated with a Hathor head. Two kinds of sistrum are attested, [naos](/wiki/Naos_(hieroglyph) "Naos (hieroglyph)")-shaped and hoop-shaped; the latter became the more common.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson2008230-134)

## Funerary art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Funerary art")]

See also: [Funerary art § Ancient Egypt and Nubia](/wiki/Funerary_art#Ancient_Egypt_and_Nubia "Funerary art")

### Coffins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Coffins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Detail_of_Tutankhamun%27s_middle_coffin.jpg/330px-Detail_of_Tutankhamun%27s_middle_coffin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detail_of_Tutankhamun%27s_middle_coffin.jpg)

Detail of the middle coffin of [Tutankhamun](/wiki/Tutankhamun "Tutankhamun"); 1355–1346 BC; wood, [gold leaf](/wiki/Gold_leaf "Gold leaf"), semi-precious stones and inlaid glass; 39 × 12 × 12 cm; [Egyptian Museum](/wiki/Egyptian_Museum,_Cairo "Egyptian Museum, Cairo") (Cairo). The king is shown in the Osiride form, with arms crossed over the chest and holding the [crook and flail](/wiki/Crook_and_flail "Crook and flail") symbols of power and authority.

The earliest purpose-built funerary containers for bodies were simple rectangular wooden boxes, attested in the [1st Dynasty](/wiki/First_Dynasty_of_Egypt "First Dynasty of Egypt"). A coffin swiftly became an essential part of the burial equipment. Known euphemistically as the "lord of life", its primary function was to provide a home for the Ka and to protect the physical body from harm. In the [4th Dynasty](/wiki/Fourth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Fourth Dynasty of Egypt"), the development of longer coffins allowed the body to be buried fully extended (rather than curled up on its side in a foetal position). At the end of the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Old_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Old Kingdom of Egypt"), it became customary once more for the body to be laid on its side. The side of the coffin that faced east in the tomb was decorated with a pair of eyes so that the deceased could look out towards the rising sun with its promise of daily rebirth. Coffins also began to be decorated on the outside with bands of funerary texts, while pictures of food and drink offerings were painted on the inside to provide a magical substitute for the real provisions placed in the tomb.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the [First Intermediate Period](/wiki/First_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "First Intermediate Period of Egypt"), decorated coffins became a substitute for tomb decoration; in the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"), coffin texts made their first appearance, sometimes accompanied by detailed maps of the underworld. Middle Kingdom coffins show a number of distinct regional styles, echoing the cultural fragmentation of the preceding period. In the [17th](/wiki/Seventeenth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Seventeenth Dynasty of Egypt") and early [18th Dynasties](/wiki/Eighteenth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt"), the Theban area produced characteristic anthropoid *rishi* (feathered) coffins. These were replaced (except for kings) by other styles of anthropoid coffins which became the standard form throughout the country for the remainder of Egyptian history. The predominance of decorated tombs in the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") removed the need of object friezes, so coffins were generally undecorated on the inside. However, this situation was reversed again in the [Third Intermediate Period](/wiki/Third_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "Third Intermediate Period of Egypt") when new types of coffin decoration focused on the [Osiris](/wiki/Osiris "Osiris") myth and extracts from the *[Book of the Dead](/wiki/Book_of_the_Dead "Book of the Dead")*, to aid the resurrection of the deceased. In the [Ptolemaic](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom") and [Roman](/wiki/Egypt_(Roman_province) "Egypt (Roman province)") periods, a cartonnage mask was often fixed directly onto the mummy wrappings as a substitute for a coffin.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Coffins were generally made of wood; those of high-status individuals used fine quality, imported [cedar](/wiki/Cedar_wood "Cedar wood"). From the Middle Kingdom onward, wealthy individuals were often provided with a set of two or three nested coffins. The most sumptuous coffins might be inlaid with glass or precious stones, while royal coffins were often made from gold or silver.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson200853,_54_&_55-135)

### Canopic jars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Canopic jars")]

Main article: [Canopic jar](/wiki/Canopic_jar "Canopic jar")

Canopic jars are vessels which were used for storing the internal organs removed during mummification. These were named after the human-headed jars that were worshiped as personifications of Kanops (the helmsman of [Menelaus](/wiki/Menelaus "Menelaus") in [Greek mythology](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology")) by the inhabitants of ancient Canopus. The practice of evisceration is first attested in the burial of [Hetepheres](/wiki/Hetepheres "Hetepheres")[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] in the early [4th Dynasty](/wiki/Fourth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Fourth Dynasty of Egypt"). Her organs were stored in a [travertine](/wiki/Travertine "Travertine") [canopic chest](/wiki/Canopic_chest "Canopic chest") divided into four compartments. Later, each organ – the [liver](/wiki/Liver "Liver"), [lungs](/wiki/Lung "Lung"), [stomach](/wiki/Stomach "Stomach") and [intestines](/wiki/Intestine "Intestine") – was provided with a separate jar, of stone or pottery, and placed under the symbolic protection of one of the [Four sons of Horus](/wiki/Four_sons_of_Horus "Four sons of Horus"). During the [First Intermediate Period](/wiki/First_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "First Intermediate Period of Egypt"), the stoppers of canopic jars began to be modeled in the form of human heads. From the late [18th Dynasty](/wiki/Eighteenth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt"), they were more commonly modelled to resemble the heads of the protecting genii ([baboon](/wiki/Baboon "Baboon"), [jackal](/wiki/Jackal "Jackal"), [falcon](/wiki/Falcon "Falcon") and human). This became the standard for canopic equipment in the [19th Dynasty](/wiki/Nineteenth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Nineteenth Dynasty of Egypt"). In the [Third Intermediate Period](/wiki/Third_Intermediate_Period_of_Egypt "Third Intermediate Period of Egypt"), the mummified organs were generally returned to the body, but wealthy burials could still include a dummy set of jars. The last known royal set of canopic jars were made of [Apries](/wiki/Apries "Apries"). The manufacture of canopic equipment continued into the [Ptolemaic Period](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Period "Ptolemaic Period") but ceased by Roman times.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson200848-136)

### Masks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Masks")]

Funerary masks have been used at all periods. Examples range from the gold masks of [Tutankhamun](/wiki/Tutankhamun "Tutankhamun") and [Psusennes I](/wiki/Psusennes_I "Psusennes I") to the [Roman "mummy portraits"](/wiki/Fayum_mummy_portraits "Fayum mummy portraits") from [Hawara](/wiki/Hawara "Hawara") and the [Fayum](/wiki/Fayum "Fayum"). Whether in a funerary or religious context, the purpose of a mask was the same: to transform the wearer from a mortal to a divine state.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson2008144-137)

### Ushabti

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Ushabti")]

Main article: [Ushabti](/wiki/Ushabti "Ushabti")

[Ushabtis](/wiki/Ushabti "Ushabti") (a.k.a. shawabti or shabti) are funerary figurines. Their purpose was to act as a substitute for the deceased when he was called upon to perform agricultural work or corvée labor in the afterlife. Ushabtis evolved in the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt") from the servant statues included among [grave goods](/wiki/Grave_goods "Grave goods"). The earliest examples were crude statuettes in wax, clay or wood; later, they were fashioned as mummiform figures and, from the end of the [12th Dynasty](/wiki/Twelfth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Twelfth Dynasty of Egypt"), they were customarily inscribed with the "ushabti text" (chapter 6 of the *[Book of the dead](/wiki/Book_of_the_dead "Book of the dead")* which specifies the ushabti's duties).[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson2008226-138)

## Art of Meroë

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Art of Meroë")]

See also: [Meroë](/wiki/Mero%C3%AB "Meroë")

[Ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") shared a long and complex history with the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile") Valley to the south, the region called [Nubia](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia") (modern [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan")). Beginning with the [Kerma culture](/wiki/Kerma_culture "Kerma culture") and continuing with the [Kingdom of Kush](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kush "Kingdom of Kush") based at [Napata](/wiki/Napata "Napata") and then [Meroë](/wiki/Mero%C3%AB "Meroë"), Nubian culture absorbed Egyptian influences at various times, for both political and religious reasons. The result is a rich and complex visual culture.

The artistic production of Meroë reflects a range of influences. First, it was an indigenous African culture with roots stretching back thousands of years. To this is added the fact that the wealth of Meroë was based on trade with Egypt when it was ruled by the [Ptolemaic dynasty](/wiki/Ptolemaic_dynasty "Ptolemaic dynasty") (332–330 BC) and the [Romans](/wiki/Egypt_(Roman_province) "Egypt (Roman province)") (30 BC – 395 AD), so Hellenistic and Roman objects and ideas were imported, as well as Egyptian influences.

## Egyptian Revival art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_of_ancient_Egypt&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Egyptian Revival art")]

Main articles: [Egyptian Revival decorative arts](/wiki/Egyptian_Revival_decorative_arts "Egyptian Revival decorative arts") and [Egyptian Revival architecture](/wiki/Egyptian_Revival_architecture "Egyptian Revival architecture")

See also: [Ancient Egypt in the Western imagination](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt_in_the_Western_imagination "Ancient Egypt in the Western imagination")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Place_du_Caire.jpg/250px-Place_du_Caire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Place_du_Caire.jpg)

Foire du Caire building (1828), from Paris, an early manifestation of Egyptian Revivalism: façade adorned with heads of the Egyptian goddess [Hathor](/wiki/Hathor "Hathor")

Egyptian Revival art is a style in Western art, mainly of the early nineteenth century, in which Egyptian motifs were applied to a wide variety of [decorative arts](/wiki/Decorative_arts "Decorative arts") objects. It was underground in American decorative arts throughout the nineteenth century, continuing into the 1920s. The major motifs of Egyptian art, such as [obelisks](/wiki/Obelisk "Obelisk"), [hieroglyphs](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyph "Egyptian hieroglyph"), the sphinx, and pyramids, were used in various artistic media, including architecture, furniture, ceramics, and silver. Egyptian motifs provided an exotic alternative to the more traditional styles of the day. Over the course of the nineteenth century, American tastes evolved from a highly ornamented aesthetic to a simpler, sparer sense of decoration; the vocabulary of ancient Egyptian art would be interpreted and adapted in different ways depending on the standards and motivations of the time.[[138]](#cite_note-139) This legacy endures in contemporary Egyptian art, as seen in the work of Rasha Amin, whose Cairo upbringing shapes her multidisciplinary pieces, reflecting the influence of Egypt’s rich cultural heritage.[[139]](#cite_note-140)

Enthusiasm for the artistic style of Ancient Egypt is generally attributed to the excitement over [Napoleon's conquest of Egypt](/wiki/French_campaign_in_Egypt_and_Syria "French campaign in Egypt and Syria") and, in Britain, to Admiral Nelson's defeat of Napoleon at the [Battle of the Nile](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Nile "Battle of the Nile") in 1798. Napoleon took a scientific expedition with him to Egypt. Publication of the expedition's work, the *[Description de l'Égypte](/wiki/Description_de_l%27%C3%89gypte "Description de l'Égypte")*, began in 1809 and came out in a series though 1826, inspiring everything from sofas with sphinxes for legs, to tea sets painted with the pyramids. It was the popularity of the style that was new, Egyptianizing works of art had appeared in scattered European settings from the time of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_art "Renaissance art").
